summary: Gruber challenges the widespread belief that Android's growing market share will inevitably translate into developer dominance over iOS. He argues that the 'Church of Market Share' treats all users as equivalent, when in reality iPhone users are demonstrably more engaged — they browse more, buy more apps, and use more apps. He points to Apple's extraordinary profit share (two-thirds of handset profits from just 5% of unit sales) as evidence that profit share matters more than market share. He contends that market share can be artificially inflated by selling at cost, while profits are the sustainable fuel for business. A year after asking where Android's killer apps are, he notes that Android's market share growth has not translated into a corresponding developer ecosystem advantage.
key_points: The assumption that Android's market share lead will inevitably produce a developer share lead is an article of faith, not an established fact
iPhone users are measurably different from Android users — they browse more, spend more on apps, and install more apps, making them more valuable to developers
Apple earns two-thirds of handset industry profits from only 5% of total handset sales, demonstrating that profit share and market share are decoupled
Market share can be bought by selling at or below cost, but that strategy is unsustainable — profits are the fuel that keeps a business running
Despite a year of Android market share growth, the iOS developer ecosystem advantage remained largely unchanged
money_quotes: It's an article of faith in the Church of Market Share that Android is nearing a tipping point where its market share lead will inevitably turn into a developer share lead, too.
To think that market share alone is a primary motivation for all or even most of the developers who've turned the iOS App Store into a phenomenon is to miss the forest for the trees.
What I see as the fundamental flaw in the Church of Market Share doctrine is the assumption that users are users.
What's more important than a company's share of the overall market is the company's share of the profitable side of the overall market.
You can say that it's elitist or arrogant to argue that iOS users are better customers than Android users. But you can also say that it's the truth.
A company with deep pockets and a willingness to spend can buy market share by selling products at or even below cost. You can cheat your way to market share.
A fire can't keep burning without fuel, and profits are the fuel for business.
Market share is definitely something, but it's not everything.
key_insight: Market share is a misleading metric when users are not equivalent — Apple's dominance in profit share and developer engagement proves that the quality of your customer base matters more than its size.
